items.q: Where is almost all the mass of an atom located?
items.correct: In the nucleus, made up of protons and neutrons.
items.misconception: Electrons contribute significantly to the mass of an atom.
items.random_wrong: Mass is spread evenly throughout the entire atom including the electron shells.
items.why: Students see electrons listed alongside protons and neutrons and assume they contribute comparable mass — in reality an electron has ~1/1836 the mass of a proton.

items.q: What is different between two isotopes of the same element?
items.correct: They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
items.misconception: Isotopes have different numbers of electrons, which is why their mass differs.
items.random_wrong: Isotopes are atoms of different elements with the same mass.
items.why: Students conflate isotopes with ions because both alter a property of the atom — but ions change electrons (charge), isotopes change neutrons (mass).

items.q: What happens to electrons when sodium reacts with chlorine?
items.correct: Sodium loses one electron to chlorine; the resulting Na+ and Cl- ions attract electrostatically.
items.misconception: Sodium and chlorine share an electron between them.
items.random_wrong: Protons move from chlorine to sodium to balance the charges.
items.why: Students over-apply the covalent (sharing) model to all bonding because sharing is introduced first and feels intuitive.

items.q: Why are alloys harder than pure metals?
items.why: It's different sized atoms distort layers. “smaller atoms only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which has the largest surface area to volume ratio?
items.why: Correct answer: a 100 nm cube. “a 1 cm cube” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A cube of side 10 nm has surface area to volume ratio in nm^-1?
items.why: The real figure is 0.6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
